data.title: India's central bank flags concerns in unsecured lending, capital markets frenzy
data.description:
India's central bank has seen concerns of over-leveraging in unsecured lending and frenzy in capital markets, Deputy Governor Rajeshwar Rao said on Friday.

Rao, who is currently in charge of monetary policy among other departments at the Reserve Bank of India, said there was a need to ensure that excess financialisation through technology does not lead to over-leveraging.

data.title: Copart Inc reports results for the quarter ended January 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Copart Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 40 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended January 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 33 cents. The mean expectation of seven analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 37 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 34 cents to 38 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 14% to $1.16 billion from a year ago; analysts expected $1.13 billion.

  • Copart Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 40 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $387.4 million.

  • Copart Inc shares had risen by 1.4% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 0.5% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no negative revisions of earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 7 "strong buy" or "buy," 4 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

  • The average consensus recommendation for the online services peer group is also "buy"

Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Copart Inc is $64.00

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data February 21 at 05:22 a.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. Moody's cuts Nissan rating to junk status, keeps negative outlook
data.description:
Moody's Ratings said on Friday it has downgraded its rating of Nissan Motor's credit by one notch to junk status, citing a weak and worsening outlook for the Japanese automaker's credit profile.

The credit-rating firm cut Nissan's senior unsecured rating to Ba1 from Baa3 and maintained its negative outlook.

In a report, senior analyst Dean Enjo cited "risks associated with the implementation of its new restructuring plan, the renewal of its aging product range and global trade policies".

Nissan has been working on a turnaround programme under which it plans to cut its workforce by 9,000 people and global manufacturing capacity by 20% as it struggles with worse-than-expected performance in key markets the U.S. and China.

Moody's said it expected the free cash flow of Nissan's car business, which has turned negative in the current fiscal year, to remain in the red throughout the fiscal year starting from April.

The automaker's cash flow recovery was at risk from the current global trade environment with potential U.S. import tariffs on its sizeable production base in Mexico, Moody's said.

It added that Nissan's automotive business had substantial cash holdings that would provide sufficient liquidity for its negative free cash flow and debt maturities over the next 12 months.

Last month, S&P Global Ratings cut the automaker's credit outlook to negative and affirmed its BB+ rating.

Last week, Nissan ended talks with Honda Motor for a possible merger, pitching it deeper into uncertainty. It said at that time it would provide an update on the turnaround programme within a month.

data.title: Sika Reports Higher FY24 Attributable Net Profit, Net Sales
data.description:
Sika said Friday its net profit and net sales for the full year 2024 increased year over year.

Net profit attributable to shareholders for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 1.25 billion francs, compared with 1.06 billion francs earlier. EPS moved to 7.76 francs from 6.65 francs.

The Swiss specialty chemicals group's net sales were 11.76 billion francs, compared with 11.24 billion francs a year ago.

Analysts polled by FactSet were expecting 1.22 billion francs in net income and 7.79 francs in EPS.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a dividend of 3.60 francs per share for the period. It paid 3.30 francs per share a year ago.

For the full year 2025, the company expects sales to grow in the 3% to 6% range in local currencies. The group also affirmed its 2028 strategic mid-term target for profitable growth.

data.title: Mosel Vitelic to Pay Dividend
data.description:
Mosel Vitelic to distribute NT$0.3 per share in cash dividends for the second half of 2024, totaling NT$47.22 million, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The chip manufacturer did not disclose other details.

data.title: Ice hockey-Canada beat US to win 4 Nations filled with political tension
data.description:
Canada beat fierce rivals the United States 3-2 in overtime to win a riveting 4 Nations Face-Off final in Boston on Thursday and close out an ice hockey tournament high on political tension.

Connor McDavid scored the winner after much-maligned Canadian goalie Jordan Binnington had delivered some clutch saves.

The taut action on the ice mirrored the geopolitical tensions bubbling in North America since U.S. President Donald Trump took office last month.

Trump, who has spoken openly about his desire to absorb America's northern neighbor as its "51st state" and has threatened huge tariffs on Canadian imports, called the U.S. team to offer his support ahead of the contest.

Canadian Prime Minster Justin Trudeau bit back after the game.

"You can't take our country - and you can't take our game," he posted on social media.

Despite the public address announcer requesting respect for both team's national anthems, many U.S. fans booed during the pre-game rendition of "O Canada" at TD Garden.

Canadian fans had jeered the U.S. national anthem at last week's game in Montreal, which the Americans won 3-1.

While Saturday's emotionally charged match-up saw three fights break out in the opening nine seconds, there were fewer skirmishes on Thursday but no shortage of pressure.

"Just to see the reaction, to know what it means to us," McDavid said on the ice when asked what the most gratifying thing about the win was.

"I know it's just a quick tournament and it's not an Olympic gold medal or anything like that, but it means the world to our group as you can see. Everybody battled so hard all week.

"It was special."

The inaugural edition of the tournament also featured Finland and Sweden.

Canada drew first blood when Nathan MacKinnon scored through traffic before Auston Matthews wrapped around goal and Brady Tkachuk jammed home the puck to make it 1-1.

The U.S. took the lead in the second period through Jake Sanderson before Canada's Sam Bennett leveled and a scoreless third period set up a furious five-on-five overtime, where McDavid emerged the hero for Canada.

The tournament, which the NHL presented in lieu of its traditional All-Star game, has been hugely successful with Saturday's game between the U.S. and Canada drawing 10.1 million viewers, according to the NHL.

Thursday's numbers are expected to be even higher, both in terms of viewership and sports betting.

data.title: Mosel Vitelic Unit Decides Against Paying Dividend
data.description:
Mosel Vitelic's unit Mou Fu Investment unit will not distribute dividends for 2024 following a board resolution on Thursday, according to a same-day Taiwan Exchange filing.

The company did not disclose any details regarding the decision.

Hang Seng Index May Consolidate in Near Term — Market Talk
data.description: The Hang Seng Index may consolidate in the near term after a more than 20% rally since mid-January, say BNP Paribas analysts in a note. The analysts see event risk around earnings season for the index heavyweight and the National People's Congress meeting in early March. The current positioning in China onshore equities is close to a three-year high, showing a potential risk of profit-taking, they add. Investors may consider replacing long delta-1 holdings with a short-dated call spread or call ratio on the Hang Seng Index, which offer limited downside exposure while allowing investors to maintain upside participation within a range, they say.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Beijing Jingneng Power Raises Stake in Daihai New Energy to 90%
data.description:
Beijing Jingneng Power acquired a 39% stake in Daihai New Energy for 949.3 million yuan via a public bidding process.

This increases the power producer's ownership to 90% from 51%, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse

Daihai New Energy operates a large-scale wind and solar power project.

Shares of Beijing Jingneng Power were down less than 1% in recent trade.

Australia's Federal Court Finds Telstra Misled Customers Over Broadband Speed Reductions
data.description:
Australia's Federal Court ruled on Friday that Telstra misled nearly 9,000 customers by failing to disclose a reduction in their broadband upload speeds, following legal action by the regulator, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) said in a Friday statement.

In October and November 2020, Telstra migrated 8,897 customers who were on a Belong National Broadband Network plan with a maximum upload speed of 40 megabits per second, to a service with a maximum upload speed of 20Mbps, the regulator added.

"There was no reduction to the price Telstra charged its customers even though the cost charged by NBN Co to Telstra was AU$7 a month less for the new, lower speed service.", said ACCC Commissioner Liza Carver.

Telstra admitted to telling 2,785 customers that they were receiving a maximum upload speed of 40Mbps when they were not, the statement added.

The telecommunications firm acknowledged its failure to disclose the migration to its customers in 2021 and provided a one-off AU$90 credit to the affected consumers.

The ACCC said it is seeking declarations, penalties, consumer redress, costs, and other orders and the court will determine the penalty and any consumer redress after a hearing on a date to be fixed.

Telstra has previously paid AU$15 million in penalties after being found guilty of making misleading promotions about internet speed plans in 2021.

Telstra did not immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

data.title: CMC Magnetics Disposes of Dynapack International Technology Shares for Nearly NT$352 Million
data.description:
CMC Magnetics sold 1,922,000 shares of Dynapack International Technology (TWO:3211) for NT$351.5 million, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The shares were sold at an average price of NT$182.9 apiece between Feb. 10 and Feb. 20, the optical storage media and peripheral products manufacturer said.

India Services Growth Hits 11-Month High
data.description:
The HSBC India Services PMI increased to 61.1 in February 2025, up from 56.5 in the previous months, which was the softest pace since November 2022, preliminary estimates showed.

Still, the latest figure marked the 43rd consecutive month of growth in services activity and the fastest pace since March 2024, as output growth accelerated while new orders continued to increase, with foreign sales rising faster.

Meanwhile, employment increased at a faster rate.

On the price front, input price inflation accelerated due to higher food prices.

Finally, business sentiment remained optimistic.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Standard Chartered Net Profit Falls, Announces $1.5 Billion Buyback

The London-based bank, which generates much of its profit in Asia, said underlying profit before tax declined 0.9% to $1.05 billion..

Funding Cuts at CFPB Seen Leading to 'Regulatory Vacuum' for Big Banks

Turmoil at the agency could harm not only consumers but also small state-chartered banks, as their bigger rivals gain advantage from lax CFPB supervision, 23 state attorneys general say in court filing.

Lloyds Banking's Profit Dented by Extra Motor-Finance Provision

The lender took a further provision in the fourth quarter for a potential hit linked to its motor finance business, leading its pretax profit to miss expectations.

IRS Begins to Fire 6,000 Workers, Threatening Tax-Season Delays

Some collections and audits are likely to be paused, and one-on-one personal service is set to be more limited.

Ryan Cohen Boosts Alibaba Stake to $1 Billion

Ryan Cohen still has his sights set on Alibaba Group and has been building up his position in the Chinese e-commerce behemoth.

Synapse Executive Alerted Accountants Before $100 Million Missing Funds Scandal

The former company official testified before a New York grand jury investigating fraud and other possible felonies.

Zurich Estimates $200 Million in Losses From California Wildfires

The insurer expects that the wildfires will lead to $200 million in pretax losses for the group, which reported better-than-expected results for 2024.

HSBC Plans New Share Buyback, Cost Cuts

The London-based bank's chief executive is concentrating on streamlining the lender's businesses and reducing costs.

A Lingering Question at Coinbase: Who Really Owns the Customers' Crypto?

Coinbase's accounting change significantly affects its numbers and highlights an old worry.

Ackman Boosts Bid for Howard Hughes, Vowing to Create Modern Version of Berkshire Hathaway

The revised proposal would increase Pershing Square's stake to 48%.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Oil prices end higher as investors shake off rise in U.S. crude inventories

Oil futures ended higher Thursday, with investors shaking off a larger-than-expected rise in U.S. crude inventories.

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Rise More Than Expected

U.S. crude oil inventories increased by more than expected last week in a fourth consecutive build, while product stocks declined, according to U.S. government data.

U.S. Natural Gas Snaps 7-Session Winning Streak

U.S. natural gas futures snapped a seven-session winning streak as near-term forecasts point to milder weather to follow this week's Arctic blast.

How Tariffs Could Shock America's Power System

Transformers used in power grids are especially vulnerable to trade disruptions.

Schneider Electric Beats Market Views

The energy-management and automation group posted higher net profit and revenue for 2024 on continued strength in energy management, beating market views.

Activist Elliott to Seek Phillips 66 Board Seats

With a more-than-$2.5 billion stake in Phillips 66, Elliott has pushed for a streamlining of the oil refiner and seeks more representation on the company's board.

Repsol Swings to Loss on Production, Energy Price Declines

Repsol reported a decline in earnings, swinging to a quarterly loss, after production and energy prices fell in the final quarter of last year

Occidental Stock Drops After Mixed Results. What's Dragging on Buffett's Favorite.

OXY stock has lost 19% over the past year. Weak crude prices are to blame.

Diamondback Energy to Acquire Double Eagle

The oil and natural gas company reached an agreement with Double Eagle in exchange for 6.9 million shares of its common stock and $3 billion in cash.

Galp Misses Expectations; Launches Buyback

The oil-and-gas producer launched a $262 million share buyback and said its results reflected a strong performance despite a volatile economic environment.

data.title: Haffner Energy, Atoba Energy Partner to Scale Sustainable Aviation Fuel Production
data.description:
Haffner Energy entered a strategic partnership with Atoba Energy to accelerate sustainable aviation fuel project financing and development.

The French renewable energy producer said Thursday the French sustainable aviation fuel aggregator will help secure long-term offtake deals on projects, including the Paris-Vatry program in France, which aims for full-scale production by 2030 to coincide with the next phase of the European sustainable aviation fuel mandate.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0247 GMT - Palm oil prices rise in Asian trading, tracking gains in soybean oil overnight on the Chicago Board of Trade. Palm oil's recent rally has pushed it to a premium over soybean oil, which could weigh on demand, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. That trend may persist amid expectations of a strong U.S. soy harvest, it adds. Technically, with crude palm oil futures continuing to signal bullish momentum, prices may test the MYR4,750-a-ton resistance level, with pullbacks offering buying opportunities, it says. AmInvestment Bank sees support for CPO prices at 4,613 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,747 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for May delivery rises 62 ringgit to 4,704 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0245 GMT - Iron-ore prices are higher in early Asian trade amid tight supply. The Australian cyclone likely affected 7 million tons of iron-ore shipment, weighing on its supply, Nanhua Futures says in a research note. On the demand front, market sentiment is improving on the prospect of better macroeconomic performance in China, it adds. Investors will pay attention to China's National People's Congress in early March for any further property supportive measures. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is up 1.6% higher at 839.50 yuan a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0233 GMT - Aluminum edges higher in early Asian trade on reports that the European Union has backed a ban on Russian aluminum, say ANZ research analysts in a commentary. EU Ambassadors have agreed to move forward with a fresh package of sanctions against the key supplier, the analysts say noting media reports. These include a gradual ban on imports, restrictions on dozens of Russian vessels and prohibiting about a dozen banks from accessing the SWIFT international payment system, the analysts add. The three-month LME Aluminum contract is 0.1% higher at $2,729.50 a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0024 GMT - Gold edges higher in early Asian trade. The ongoing conflict between Russia and Ukraine adds a layer of complexity, says Inki Cho, Exness financial markets strategist consultant, noting that the U.S. began negotiations with Russia, raising hopes for a resolution. Steady progress in negotiations could strengthen risk appetite and apply pressure on the precious metal, Cho says in a note. Any setbacks could also reinforce gold's appeal as a safe-haven asset, Cho adds. Spot gold is up 0.1% at $2941.26/oz.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

2157 GMT - Texas Roadhouse increased its commodity inflation expectations for 2025 to a 3% to 4% increase from 2024 levels, up from a 2% to 3% previously expected, largely due to rising beef prices. The casual-dining chain said in a 4Q earnings call that it hasn't seen herders retaining enough cattle for breeding, fueling its expectations that its main commodity will keep increasing in price. The chain posted a 7.7% 4Q same-store sales increase, and chain's shares rose 1.3% in after-market trading. (heather.haddon@wsj.com; @heatherhaddon)

2018 GMT - The march toward $3,000 continues for gold, with the front-month contract settling up 0.7% to a record $2,940 per troy ounce. Global economic uncertainty continues to provide upside support for gold. While the inflation picture has improved worldwide, it remains an issue, and President Trump's tariff threats add volatility, although analysts hope they are still only a leverage tool. "We await further gyrations in the Administration's tariff policies, but still believe that, at least in part, Trump will use tariffs as a negotiating tool to motivate countries to cooperate with his key priorities," Citi says in a note. SPDR Gold gains 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1952 GMT - Lean hog futures on the CME settle down 1.3% to 88.6 cents a pound, continuing the trend where lower pork cutout prices put pressure on futures. Carcass cutouts reported by the USDA yesterday fell by $3.95 per hundredweight to $95.52 per cwt. Cutouts fell another $1.28 per cwt through midday. Live cattle futures finished down 0.5% to $1.9385 a pound, ahead of the USDA's Cattle on Feed report scheduled to be released at 3 p.m. eastern time tomorrow. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1920 GMT - Crude futures hold onto moderate gains as near-term supply concerns are partly offset by a bigger-than-expected 4.6 million barrel increase in U.S. crude stockpiles despite a drop in net imports. "April WTI crude oil is seemingly willing to discount the big build, the EIA premium to the API, and the Line 16 adjustment number and instead concentrate on those rather impressive import/export dynamics," Mizuho's Robert Yawger say in a note.While imports fell by 488,000 barrels a day last week, exports grew by 472,000 b/d. March WTI goes off the board at $72.57 a barrel, up 0.4%, and the April contract rises 0.5% to $72.48. Brent gains 0.6% to $76.48 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1913 GMT - Some 55% more U.S. farmers declared bankruptcy in 2024 versus the previous year, although the number is still relatively low versus what was seen in the previous decade. In 2024, 216 farm bankruptcy cases were filed, says the American Farm Bureau Federation in a note, up from 139 bankruptcies in 2023. But despite the big uptick, the total number of bankruptcies is still well below the 599 bankruptcies reported in 2019 — a high point in bankruptcy cases before the disruption of the Covid-19 pandemic. But the trend is worrying, says the AFBF, especially as the USDA forecasts a decrease of crop receipts in 2025. "As the farm economy continues to be squeezed by decreased receipts and increased expenses, farm financials will be strained," says Samantha Ayoub of the AFBF. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1901 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures snap a seven-session winning streak as near-term forecasts point to milder weather to follow this week's Arctic blast. "Severe cold weather brings prices up and warming trends take prices right back down," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. This week's cold spell had more than 130 million people under wind chills in single digits or lower, he adds. The Nymex front month settles down 3% at $4.152/mmBtu in volatile trade that saw a swing between an overnight high of $4.476 and the day's low of $4.034. The EIA's storage report showed the inventory deficit versus the five-year average increasing to 118 Bcf last week from 67 Bcf a week earlier. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1835 GMT - U.S. farmers are expected to plant more acres of corn, at the expense of soybean, wheat, cotton and grain sorghum, CoBank said. Tanner Ehmke, grains and oilseeds economist with CoBank, said "While farmers tend to stick to historical crop rotations for agronomic reasons and market diversification, corn's price rally relative to other crops suggests a major shift in acreage is in the offing." A report from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange says corn acres are expected to climb 4.2%, to 94.5 million in 2025. "However, the potential for trade disputes with Canada and Mexico could significantly weaken the demand for U.S. corn and curb the increase in planted acreage," CoBank said. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1742 GMT - Relatively small sales of U.S. soybeans — which have come as Brazilian soybean producers start harvesting and refilling the coffers of the export market — are expected in tomorrow's weekly export sales report from the USDA. Analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast soybean export sales will land anywhere from 100,000 metric tons to 600,000 tons. Last week's combined total was 209,800 tons. But recent data suggest that U.S. soybeans are abundant, and prices are trending lower as a result. In an assessment, AgMarket.net says that soybean basis bids have been trending lower since the start of the year, possibly an indication of more supply availability. Soybeans are up 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Markets Digest Earnings, Data
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

UK consumer confidence, retail sales, public sector finances, France monthly business survey. Flash PMIs for France, Germany, Eurozone, UK. Trading updates for Air Liquide, Sibanye-Stillwater

Opening Call:

European stock futures were lower, after Asia stocks were mixed. The dollar held steady and the yield on U.S. Treasurys edged lower. Gold fell and oil rose.

Equities:

European stock futures pointed to a lower open as investors braced for a slew of economic data releases from the region and looked ahead to Germany's elections on Sunday.

International stocks have rallied despite concerns about tariffs and the possibility of higher inflation due to trade tension so far this year. This trend could continue even if U.S. stocks also head higher.

Jason Hunter, a technical strategist at J.P. Morgan Securities, said that the S&P 500 still has some near-term momentum and that there could be "additional upside in the weeks and months ahead," adding that the index could see an upside of 5% to around 6400 in "a late-winter/early-spring advance."

"More interesting dynamics have unfolded in other global equity markets despite the largely consensus US-exceptionalism theme that carried into the year," he said.

Forex:

The U.S. dollar held steady as investors further digested news of an expected call between Washington and Beijing.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Bessent reportedly said he'll make his first call with his Chinese counterpart today, Maybank analysts said in an FX strategy and research report.

Bessent plans to discuss how China can "rebalance its economy toward domestic consumption" as well as concerns over inflows of precursor ingredients for fentanyl from China, the analysts noted.

Bonds:

U.S. Treasury yields edged lower, with two headlines giving traders reassurance about near-term supply dynamics in the Treasury market.

The Fed minutes released on Wednesday brought an indication the central bank is considering pausing balance-sheet runoff until the debt-ceiling fight is resolved, potentially meaning the Fed's asset book may soak up more securities for longer.

This was followed by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent telling media that the U.S. is still a "long way" from shifting to more long-term debt issuance in auctions, helping stem concerns about another channel of increased supply.

Energy:

Oil rose as concerns over supply outweighed another increase in U.S. crude inventories, IG's Axel Rudolph said.

Oil prices have climbed this week amid uncertainty over production, with OPEC+ mulling a delay of an output increase and a Ukrainian drone attack reducing oil flows, the senior technical analyst added

Metals:

Gold edged fell as the ongoing conflict between Russia and Ukraine added a layer of complexity, said Inki Cho, Exness financial markets strategist consultant.

Cho noted that the U.S. began negotiations with Russia, raising hopes for a resolution. Steady progress in negotiations could strengthen risk appetite and apply pressure on the precious metal, Cho said.

Any setbacks, meanwhile, could also reinforce gold's appeal as a safe-haven asset, Cho added.

-

Aluminum edged higher on reports that the European Union has backed a ban on Russian aluminum, said ANZ research analysts.

EU Ambassadors have agreed to move forward with a fresh package of sanctions against the key supplier, the analysts noted, citing media reports.

These include a gradual ban on imports, restrictions on dozens of Russian vessels and prohibiting about a dozen banks from accessing the SWIFT international payment system, the analysts added.

-

Iron-ore prices were higher amid tight supply.

The Australian cyclone likely affected 7 million tons of iron-ore shipment, weighing on its supply, Nanhua Futures said.

On the demand front, market sentiment is improving on the prospect of better macroeconomic performance in China, it added, noting that investors are expected to focus on China's National People's Congress in early March for any further property supportive measures.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

U.K. Consumers Feel Small Boost From BOE Rate Cut, Survey Suggests

U.K. consumers were slightly less gloomy this month about expectations for their personal finances after the Bank of England cut its key interest rate, although the outlook for the economy continued to be a source of pessimism.

Research group GfK's monthly survey saw consumer confidence tick up to minus 20 in February from minus 22 in January. The result was a little better than economists' expectations for it to remain at last month's level. January's reading reflected the worst sentiment since August 2023.

U.S. Aluminum Buyers Scramble for Metal as Trump Tariff Looms

The tariffs targeting aluminum imports into the U.S. have buyers scrambling to procure the metal before the levies are expected to kick in and make imports more expensive.

Aluminum buyers - which include manufacturers of products like automobiles, beverage cans and home appliances - are attempting to stock up on primary aluminum before the Trump administration raises the 10% tariff placed on the metal to 25% starting next month.

Why Germany's Confidence Is Shattered and Its Economy Is Kaput

BERLIN-A decade ago, Germany was the model nation.

Its economy hadn't just withstood the ascendance of China; it was thriving in its wake. Its balanced public finances stood out in a world of huge government debt. And while British and U.S. lawmakers were caught up in the culture wars, German politicians continued to practice the art of compromise.

CATL, Volkswagen to Collaborate on EV Batteries Amid Intensifying Competition

Chinese battery giant Contemporary Amperex Technology will collaborate with Volkswagen on electric vehicle batteries, new materials and other auto parts as the automaker seeks to adapt to the fast-evolving and competitive EV market.

The Chinese company, in a statement released on Friday, announced that it has signed a memorandum of understanding with Volkswagen to strengthen its partnership in EV battery research and development, new material applications and the development of auto parts.

Newmont Alert to Cost Risks From Tariff-Fueled Currency Swings

Newmont is alive to the risk of rising costs at its global operations if proposed U.S. tariffs stoke volatility on currency markets, but doesn't anticipate an upheaval in sales given it mostly ships gold to buyers elsewhere.

Denver-based Newmont, the world's biggest gold producer, runs mines in places including Mexico and Canada, who President Trump has threatened with 25% tariffs as part of an aggressive trade agenda that has jolted global markets.
